n: The Oath of Fëanor
by: Fëanor and his seven sons — Maedhros, Maglor, Celegorm, Curufin, Caranthir, Amrod, Amras
on: themselves, and through them all who ever held a Silmaril
q:
Then Fëanor swore a terrible oath. His seven sons leapt straightway to his side and took the selfsame vow together, and red as blood shone their drawn swords in the glare of the torches. They swore an oath which none shall break, and none should take, by the name even of Ilúvatar, calling the Everlasting Dark upon them if they kept it not; and Manwë they named in witness, and Varda, and the hallowed mountain of Taniquetil, vowing to pursue with vengeance and hatred to the ends of the World Vala, Demon, Elf or Man as yet unborn, or any creature, great or small, good or evil, that time should bring forth unto the end of days, whoso should hold or take or keep a Silmaril from their possession.

Thus spoke Maedhros and Maglor and Celegorm, Curufin and Caranthir, Amrod and Amras, princes of the Noldor; and many quailed to hear the dread words. For so sworn, good or evil, an oath may not be broken, and it shall pursue oathkeeper and oathbreaker to the world's end.
fulfil: It drove every disaster of the First Age: the burning of the ships at Losgar, the three Kinslayings (Alqualondë, Doriath, the Havens of Sirion), the ruin of Nargothrond and Doriath, and at last the theft of the Silmarils from Eönwë's camp, after which Maedhros cast himself into a fiery chasm and Maglor cast his jewel into the Sea (Silm 24).
note: The published Silmarillion gives the oath in reported speech only. Tolkien's own verse renderings survive in the Annals of Aman (HoME X §134) and in the alliterative Flight of the Noldoli (HoME III); see entries oath_feanor_verse and oath_feanor_lay.

n: The Oath of Fëanor (verse form, Annals of Aman)
on: themselves, and all who should hold a Silmaril
q:
'Be he foe or friend, be he foul or clean,
brood of Morgoth or bright Vala,
Elda or Maia or Aftercomer,
Man yet unborn upon Middle-earth,
neither law, nor love, nor league of swords,
dread nor danger, not Doom itself,
shall defend him from Fëanor, and Fëanor's kin,
whoso hideth or hoardeth, or in hand taketh,
finding keepeth or afar casteth
a Silmaril. This swear we all:
death we will deal him ere Day's ending,
woe unto world's end! Our word hear thou,
Eru Allfather! To the everlasting
Darkness doom us if our deed faileth.
On the holy mountain hear in witness
and our vow remember, Manwë and Varda!'
fulfil: As oath_feanor_silm.
note: This is the fullest verse text Tolkien wrote of the oath, from the late Annals of Aman. It is not superseded — Christopher Tolkien simply chose the prose summary for the published Silmarillion. The names here are the older forms (Maidros, Celegorn, Cranthir, Damrod, Díriel).

n: The Oath of Fëanor (alliterative, The Flight of the Noldoli)
on: themselves, and all who should hold a Silmaril
q:
'Be he friend or foe or foul offspring
of Morgoth Bauglir, be he mortal dark
that in after days on earth shall dwell,
shall no law nor love nor league of Gods,
no might nor mercy, not moveless fate,
defend him for ever from the fierce vengeance
of the sons of Feanor, whoso seize or steal
or finding keep the fair enchanted
globes of crystal whose glory dies not,
the Silmarils. We have sworn for ever!'
c: HoME III, The Flight of the Noldoli from Valinor, ll. 132–141
fulfil: As oath_feanor_silm.
note: An early (c. 1925) unfinished alliterative poem; the wording differs from the later Annals of Aman version. Superseded as a text, though never repudiated in substance.

n: The Prophecy of the North (the Doom of the Noldor)
by: the herald of Manwë — 'some say that it was Mandos himself'
on: the House of Fëanor and all the Noldor who went on into exile
when: Y.T. 1496, on the shores of Araman
q: 'Tears unnumbered ye shall shed; and the Valar will fence Valinor against you, and shut you out, so that not even the echo of your lamentation shall pass over the mountains. On the House of Fëanor the wrath of the Valar lieth from the West unto the uttermost East, and upon all that will follow them it shall be laid also. Their Oath shall drive them, and yet betray them, and ever snatch away the very treasures that they have sworn to pursue. To evil end shall all things turn that they begin well; and by treason of kin unto kin, and the fear of treason, shall this come to pass. The Dispossessed shall they be for ever.'
fulfil: Fulfilled entire. The Oath betrayed the Fëanorians at every turn — Nargothrond, Doriath, Sirion — and the Silmarils were snatched away at the last from Maedhros and Maglor's own burning hands (Silm 24). The Nirnaeth Arnoediad, 'Unnumbered Tears', bears the doom's name.
note: The whole utterance is called both a curse and a prophecy in the text: 'the voice was heard speaking the curse and prophecy which is called the Prophecy of the North, and the Doom of the Noldor.'

n: The Curse of Mandos on the Noldor
on: all the Noldor who spilled kindred blood at Alqualondë
when: Y.T. 1496, on the shores of Araman
q: 'Ye have spilled the blood of your kindred unrighteously and have stained the land of Aman. For blood ye shall render blood, and beyond Aman ye shall dwell in Death's shadow. For though Eru appointed to you to die not in Eä, and no sickness may assail you, yet slain ye may be, and slain ye shall be: by weapon and by torment and by grief; and your houseless spirits shall come then to Mandos. There long shall ye abide and yearn for your bodies, and find little pity though all whom ye have slain should entreat for you. And those that endure in Middle-earth and come not to Mandos shall grow weary of the world as with a great burden, and shall wane, and become as shadows of regret before the younger race that cometh after. The Valar have spoken.'
fulfil: The Curse of Mandos is named repeatedly as the engine of Noldorin ruin — it 'came upon the brothers' Celegorm and Curufin in Nargothrond (Silm 19), and Ulmo warned Turgon 'that the Curse of Mandos now hastened to its fulfilment, when all the works of the Noldor should perish' (Silm 23). The fading of the Elves before Men is its long tail, running the whole length of the Third Age.
note: Continuous in the text with the Prophecy of the North; split here because the two halves do different work — the first curses Fëanor's house, this one all the kinslayers.

n: Fëanor's answer to the Doom
on: the Noldor in exile
when: Y.T. 1496, on the shores of Araman
q: 'We have sworn, and not lightly. This oath we will keep. We are threatened with many evils, and treason not least; but one thing is not said: that we shall suffer from cowardice, from cravens or the fear of cravens. Therefore I say that we will go on, and this doom I add: the deeds that we shall do shall be the matter of song until the last days of Arda.'
fulfil: True as spoken. The Silmarillion itself is the fulfilment.

n: The doom of Mandos upon Fëanor: twelve years' banishment
on: Fëanor
q: 'Thou speakest of thraldom. If thraldom it be, thou canst not escape it: for Manwë is King of Arda, and not of Aman only. And this deed was unlawful, whether in Aman or not in Aman. Therefore this doom is now made: for twelve years thou shalt leave Tirion where this threat was uttered. In that time take counsel with thyself, and remember who and what thou art. But after that time this matter shall be set in peace and held redressed, if others will release thee.'
fulfil: Fëanor went with his seven sons to Formenos, and there the Silmarils were shut in a chamber of iron — which put them where Melkor and Ungoliant could take them, and Finwë where he could be slain.
note: Fingolfin offered release ('I will release my brother'), but Fëanor 'spoke no word in answer'.

n: 'Thou hast spoken' — Fëanor's refusal to break the Silmarils
on: Fëanor
when: Y.T. 1495, after the death of the Two Trees
q:
Then he cried aloud: 'This thing I will not do of free will. But if the Valar will constrain me, then shall I know indeed that Melkor is of their kindred.'
   Then Mandos said: 'Thou hast spoken.'
fulfil: Immediately: messengers came from Formenos with the news that Melkor had slain Finwë and taken the Silmarils. Fëanor had already refused to save them.
note: Two lines earlier Fëanor says he shall be 'slain; first of all the Eldar in Aman'; Mandos answers 'Not the first' — a prophecy of Finwë's death that none present understood.

n: Fëanor's dying charge to his sons
on: Morgoth, and his own seven sons
when: F.A. 1, in the Ered Wethrin after the Dagor-nuin-Giliath
q: he cursed the name of Morgoth thrice, and laid it upon his sons to hold to their oath, and to avenge their father. Then he died; but he had neither burial nor tomb, for so fiery was his spirit that as it sped his body fell to ash, and was borne away like smoke
fulfil: His sons held to the Oath to the end — and none of them ever overthrew Thangorodrim, which Fëanor 'knew with the foreknowledge of death' they never would.
note: Reported speech; Tolkien gives no direct words for the curse itself.

n: Finrod Felagund's oath to Barahir
on: himself, toward Barahir and all his kin
when: F.A. 455 (Dagor Bragollach), in the Fen of Serech
q: he swore an oath of abiding friendship and aid in every need to Barahir and all his kin, and in token of his vow he gave to Barahir his ring.
fulfil: Ten years later Beren son of Barahir came to Nargothrond bearing the ring, and Finrod left his crown and his kingdom to go with him on the Quest of the Silmaril, and died for him in the pits of Tol-in-Gaurhoth. The Ring of Barahir passed down the line of Isildur to Aragorn.

n: 'Your oaths of faith to me you may break, but I must hold my bond'
on: himself
when: F.A. 465, in Nargothrond
q: And Felagund seeing that he was forsaken took from his head the silver crown of Nargothrond and cast it at his feet, saying: 'Your oaths of faith to me you may break, but I must hold my bond. Yet if there be any on whom the shadow of our curse has not yet fallen, I should find at least a few to follow me, and should not go hence as a beggar that is thrust from the gates.'
fulfil: Ten followed him, chief of them Edrahil. All died in Sauron's pits; Finrod last, tearing apart a werewolf with his hands and teeth to save Beren.
note: Finrod's own summary of the double bind: 'my own oath holds; and thus we are all ensnared.'

n: Celegorm's re-swearing of the Oath in Nargothrond
by: Celegorm son of Fëanor, sword drawn before the people of Nargothrond
on: any who should take a Silmaril
q: 'Be he friend or foe, whether demon of Morgoth, or Elf, or child of Men, or any other living thing in Arda, neither law, nor love, nor league of hell, nor might of the Valar, nor any power of wizardry, shall defend him from the pursuing hate of Fëanor's sons, if he take or find a Silmaril and keep it. For the Silmarils we alone claim, until the world ends.'
fulfil: Nargothrond forsook Finrod; the realm turned to stealth and ambush and 'their land was darkened'. Celegorm and Curufin were driven out, and both died in the Second Kinslaying at Doriath.
note: The clearest statement in the Silmarillion of what the Oath actually binds its swearers to.

n: Thingol's demand for a Silmaril
on: Beren son of Barahir — and, unknowingly, on Doriath itself
when: F.A. 465, in Menegroth
q: 'I see the ring, son of Barahir, and I perceive that you are proud, and deem yourself mighty. But a father's deeds, even had his service been rendered to me, avail not to win the daughter of Thingol and Melian. See now! I too desire a treasure that is withheld. For rock and steel and the fires of Morgoth keep the jewel that I would possess against all the powers of the Elf-kingdoms. Yet I hear you say that bonds such as these do not daunt you. Go your way therefore! Bring to me in your hand a Silmaril from Morgoth's crown; and then, if she will, Lúthien may set her hand in yours. Then you shall have my jewel; and though the fate of Arda lie within the Silmarils, yet you shall hold me generous.'
fulfil: 'Thus he wrought the doom of Doriath, and was ensnared within the curse of Mandos.' Beren brought the Silmaril; it drew the Oath of Fëanor into Doriath, and Thingol was slain by Dwarves over the Nauglamír that held it, and Menegroth was sacked twice.
note: Melian's answer names the trap exactly: 'you have doomed either your daughter, or yourself. And now is Doriath drawn within the fate of a mightier realm.'

n: Beren's oath to Thingol
on: himself
when: F.A. 465, in Menegroth
q: 'For little price,' he said, 'do Elven-kings sell their daughters: for gems, and things made by craft. But if this be your will, Thingol, I will perform it. And when we meet again my hand shall hold a Silmaril from the Iron Crown; for you have not looked the last upon Beren son of Barahir.'
fulfil: Kept to the letter, and cost him the hand. Returning to Menegroth he said 'It is fulfilled. Even now a Silmaril is in my hand' — and held up the right arm from which Carcharoth had bitten the hand away with the jewel inside it. From that hour he named himself Camlost, the Empty-handed.

n: 'Even now a Silmaril is in my hand'
by: Beren, before the throne of Thingol
on: himself
q:
But Beren knelt before him, and said: 'I return according to my word. I am come now to claim my own.'
    And Thingol answered: 'What of your quest, and of your vow?'
    But Beren said: 'It is fulfilled. Even now a Silmaril is in my hand.'
    Then Thingol said: 'Show it to me!'
   And Beren put forth his left hand, slowly opening its fingers; but it was empty. Then he held up his right arm; and from that hour he named himself Camlost, the Empty-handed.
fulfil: The oath was held fulfilled: Thingol yielded, and Beren took the hand of Lúthien before the throne.
note: The oath is technically true at the moment of speaking — the Silmaril was inside Carcharoth, and Beren's hand with it.

n: Huor's prophecy to Turgon: 'a new star shall arise'
on: the houses of Hador and of Fingolfin
when: F.A. 472, in the retreat from the Nirnaeth Arnoediad
q: 'Yet if it stands but a little while, then out of your house shall come the hope of Elves and Men. This I say to you, lord, with the eyes of death: though we part here for ever, and I shall not look on your white walls again, from you and from me a new star shall arise. Farewell!'
fulfil: Eärendil — son of Huor's son Tuor and of Turgon's daughter Idril — bore a Silmaril into the West and became the Star of High Hope, Gil-Estel. Literally a new star.
note: 'And Maeglin, Turgon's sister-son, who stood by, heard these words, and did not forget them' — the seed of Gondolin's betrayal.

n: Morgoth's curse upon Húrin and his kin
on: Húrin Thalion and all whom he loved — Morwen, Túrin, Niënor
when: F.A. 472, in Angband
q: 'You say it,' said Morgoth. 'I am the Elder King: Melkor, first and mightiest of all the Valar, who was before the world, and made it. The shadow of my purpose lies upon Arda, and all that is in it bends slowly and surely to my will. But upon all whom you love my thought shall weigh as a cloud of Doom, and it shall bring them down into darkness and despair. Wherever they go, evil shall arise. Whenever they speak, their words shall bring ill counsel. Whatsoever they do shall turn against them. They shall die without hope, cursing both life and death.'
fulfil: Túrin slew his friend Beleg, brought down Nargothrond by his own counsel, married his sister Niënor unknowing, and cast himself on his own sword; Niënor leapt into Cabed-en-Aras; Morwen died in the rain on the Stone of the Hapless. Every clause held.
note: Nearly identical text in Unfinished Tales, 'Narn i Hîn Húrin' (t1_d_unfinished_tales_of_numenor_and_middle_earth.txt:2123). The published Silmarillion gives only a summary.

n: 'Sit now there' — the binding of Húrin on Thangorodrim
on: Húrin Thalion
q: 'Sit now there; and look out upon the lands where evil and despair shall come upon those whom thou lovest. Thou hast dared to mock me, and to question the power of Melkor, Master of the fates of Arda. Therefore with my eyes thou shalt see, and with my ears thou shalt hear; and never shalt thou move from this place until all is fulfilled unto its bitter end.'
fulfil: 'And even so it came to pass; but it is not said that Húrin asked ever of Morgoth either mercy or death, for himself or for any of his kin.' He sat twenty-eight years and watched his children destroy each other, and was released only when the tale was done.
note: The Children of Húrin gives a slightly different text ending 'and nothing shall be hidden from you' (t1_f_the_children_of_hurin.txt:1223).

n: Mîm's curse on Andróg
on: Andróg, the outlaw whose arrow killed Mîm's son Khîm
when: F.A. 486, at Bar-en-Danwedh on Amon Rûdh
q: 'I hear you,' he said. 'You speak like a dwarf-lord of old; and at that I marvel. Now my heart is cooled, though it is not glad. My own ransom I will pay, therefore: you may dwell here, if you will. But this I will add: he that loosed the shaft shall break his bow and his arrows and lay them at my son's feet; and he shall never take an arrow nor bear bow again. If he does, he shall die by it. That curse I lay on him.'
fulfil: Andróg took up a bow again the next spring and was struck by a poisoned orc-arrow; Beleg healed him, and Mîm said only 'it will bite again'. At the sack of Amon Rûdh, Andróg 'fell mortally wounded by an arrow at the head of the outside stair'.
note: Andróg answered with a counter-curse of his own: 'The curse of a dwarf never dies, they say; but a Man's too may come home. May he die with a dart in his throat!' Mîm was in the end slain by Húrin — with a sword, not a dart.

n: 'Master of doom by doom mastered' — Niënor's naming of Túrin
on: Túrin Turambar
when: F.A. 499, at Cabed-en-Aras
q: 'Farewell, O twice beloved! A Túrin Turambar turún'ambartanen: master of doom by doom mastered! O happy to be dead!'
fulfil: She threw herself into the ravine; Túrin fell on Gurthang, which answered him and slew him. Both were named on the Stone of the Hapless above Cabed Naeramarth.
note: Túrin had taken the name Turambar, 'Master of Doom', in defiance of Morgoth's curse. Niënor's words turn it inside out. The same words stand in Silm 21 (t1_a_the_silmarillion.txt:10602).

n: Ulmo's warning to Turgon at Vinyamar
by: Ulmo, Lord of Waters — repeated by Tuor in Gondolin
on: Turgon and Gondolin
when: spoken Y.T./F.A. 64 at Vinyamar; recalled F.A. 495
q: 'Love not too well the work of thy hands and the devices of thy heart; and remember that the true hope of the Noldor lieth in the West, and cometh from the Sea.'
fulfil: Turgon would not leave Gondolin. Maeglin betrayed the city, and it fell in F.A. 510. But the hope did come from the Sea: Eärendil, born in Gondolin, sailed West and won the aid of the Valar.
note: Tuor bore Ulmo's later message that 'the Curse of Mandos now hastened to its fulfilment, when all the works of the Noldor should perish'. A fuller version stands in The Fall of Gondolin (t1_g_the_fall_of_gondolin.txt:4806).

n: The errand of Eärendil and the judgement of Manwë
by: Eärendil (the errand); Mandos, Ulmo and Manwë (the judgement)
on: Eärendil, Elwing, and their sons — and through them the Two Kindreds
when: F.A. 542, in Valimar
q:
It is told among the Elves that after Eärendil had departed, seeking Elwing his wife, Mandos spoke concerning his fate; and he said: 'Shall mortal Man step living upon the undying lands, and yet live?' But Ulmo said: 'For this he was born into the world. And say unto me: whether is he Eärendil Tuor's son of the line of Hador, or the son of Idril, Turgon's daughter, of the Elven-house of Finwë?' And Mandos answered: 'Equally the Noldor, who went wilfully into exile, may not return hither.'
    But when all was spoken, Manwë gave judgement, and he said: 'In this matter the power of doom is given to me. The peril that he ventured for love of the Two Kindreds shall not fall upon Eärendil, nor shall it fall upon Elwing his wife, who entered into peril for love of him; but they shall not walk again ever among Elves or Men in the Outer Lands. And this is my decree concerning them: to Eärendil and to Elwing, and to their sons, shall be given leave each to choose freely to which kindred their fates shall be joined, and under which kindred they shall be judged.'
fulfil: The pardon was granted, the Host of the West came to Beleriand, and Morgoth was cast into the Void. Elrond chose the Firstborn; Elros chose Men and became the first King of Númenor. Eärendil sails the sky with the Silmaril and 'came never back to the lands of weeping and of war'.
note: This is the Ban of the Valar in its original sense — the interdict on the exiled Noldor returning to Aman — set aside by Manwë's power of doom for Eärendil alone.

n: 'Who shall release us?' — Maedhros and Maglor on the Oath
by: Maedhros and Maglor, the last sons of Fëanor
on: themselves
when: F.A. 587, after the War of Wrath
q:
And Maedhros answered: 'But how shall our voices reach to Ilúvatar beyond the Circles of the World? And by Ilúvatar we swore in our madness, and called the Everlasting Darkness upon us, if we kept not our word. Who shall release us?'
   'If none can release us,' said Maglor, 'then indeed the Everlasting Darkness shall be our lot, whether we keep our oath or break it; but less evil shall we do in the breaking.'
fulfil: Maglor yielded to Maedhros. They stole the Silmarils and the jewels burned them: Maedhros cast himself with his into a chasm of fire, Maglor cast his into the Sea and wandered the shores ever after, 'singing in pain and regret beside the waves'.
note: The clearest statement Tolkien wrote of the theology of an unbreakable oath: an oath sworn by Eru cannot be released by any power within Arda.

n: The Doom of Men and the Doom of the Elves — the Gift of Ilúvatar
by: Ilúvatar, as the Eldar report it
on: the two Kindreds, Elves and Men
when: before the Elder Days
q: It is one with this gift of freedom that the children of Men dwell only a short space in the world alive, and are not bound to it, and depart soon whither the Elves know not. Whereas the Elves remain until the end of days, and their love of the Earth and all the world is more single and more poignant therefore, and as the years lengthen ever more sorrowful. For the Elves die not till the world dies, unless they are slain or waste in grief (and to both these seeming deaths they are subject); neither does age subdue their strength, unless one grow weary of ten thousand centuries; and dying they are gathered to the halls of Mandos in Valinor, whence they may in time return. But the sons of Men die indeed, and leave the world; wherefore they are called the Guests, or the Strangers. Death is their fate, the gift of Ilúvatar, which as Time wears even the Powers shall envy. But Melkor has cast his shadow upon it, and confounded it with darkness, and brought forth evil out of good, and fear out of hope.
fulfil: Standing. It underwrites everything else in the legendarium: the Fall of Númenor, Arwen's choice, Aragorn's death.
note: The gift-and-doom is deliberately double-edged. The Númenórean rebellion turns on refusing to read it as a gift.

n: The Ban of the Valar on the Númenóreans
by: the Lords of Valinor, by the design of Manwë
on: the Dúnedain of Númenor
when: S.A. 32 onward
q: But the Lords of Valinor forbade them to sail so far westward that the coasts of Númenor could no longer be seen; and for long the Dúnedain were content, though they did not fully understand the purpose of this ban. But the design of Manwë was that the Númenóreans should not be tempted to seek for the Blessed Realm, nor desire to overpass the limits set to their bliss, becoming enamoured of the immortality of the Valar and the Eldar and the lands where all things endure.
fulfil: Held for over three thousand years — 'for they did not dare to break the Ban of the Lords of the West' — and broken at last by Ar-Pharazôn in S.A. 3319, which destroyed Númenor and removed Aman from the circles of the world.
note: Also in The Fall of Númenor (t2, Estate-approved, ed. Brian Sibley), which collects the same material.

n: 'The Doom of the World, One alone can change who made it'
on: the King of Númenor and his people
when: S.A. 2200s, in the days of Tar-Atanamir
q: 'The Doom of the World,' they said, 'One alone can change who made it. And were you so to voyage that escaping all deceits and snares you came indeed to Aman, the Blessed Realm, little would it profit you. For it is not the land of Manwë that makes its people deathless, but the Deathless that dwell therein have hallowed the land; and there you would but wither and grow weary the sooner, as moths in a light too strong and steadfast.'
fulfil: Unheeded. The Númenóreans went on to embalm their dead, worship Melkor at Sauron's teaching, and sail against Aman — where 'they were buried under falling hills' and the fleet was swallowed.
note: The Messengers' fuller answer includes the great statement 'your home is not here, neither in the Land of Aman nor anywhere within the Circles of the World' (t1_a_the_silmarillion.txt:12137).

n: Ar-Pharazôn breaks the Ban
by: Ar-Pharazôn the Golden, last King of Númenor
on: Númenor and all its people
q: Slowly the fleets passed out of the sight of the watchers in the havens, and their lights faded, and night took them; and in the morning they were gone. For a wind arose in the east and it wafted them away; and they broke the Ban of the Valar, and sailed into forbidden seas, going up with war against the Deathless, to wrest from them everlasting life within the Circles of the World.
fulfil: Immediate and total. 'Then Manwë upon the Mountain called upon Ilúvatar, and for that time the Valar laid down their government of Arda. But Ilúvatar showed forth his power, and he changed the fashion of the world' — the world was made round, Aman taken out of it, Númenor drowned, and Ar-Pharazôn's host buried alive in the Caves of the Forgotten until the Last Battle.
note: Ar-Pharazôn very nearly turned back: 'For Ar-Pharazôn wavered at the end, and almost he turned back.'

n: Isildur's curse on the Men of the Mountains
on: the King of the Mountains and his people — the Oathbreakers, the Sleepless Dead
when: S.A. 3429–3434, at the Stone of Erech
q: 'Then Isildur said to their king: ''Thou shalt be the last king. And if the West prove mightier than thy Black Master, this curse I lay upon thee and thy folk: to rest never until your oath is fulfilled. For this war will last through years uncounted, and you shall be summoned once again ere the end.'' And they fled before the wrath of Isildur, and did not dare to go forth to war on Sauron's part; and they hid themselves in secret places in the mountains and had no dealings with other men, but slowly dwindled in the barren hills.'
fulfil: Fulfilled in T.A. 3019: Aragorn summoned them at Erech, they took Pelargir and swept the Corsairs' fleet, and he released them — 'I will hold the oath fulfilled, and ye shall have peace and depart for ever.'
note: The oath they broke had been sworn to Isildur on the black Stone of Erech, brought from Númenor, 'in the beginning of the realm of Gondor'.

n: Malbeth the Seer's prophecy of the Paths of the Dead
on: the heir of Isildur and the Oathbreakers
when: T.A. 1964–1975, in the days of Arvedui
q:
Over the land there lies a long shadow,
westward reaching wings of darkness.
The Tower trembles; to the tombs of kings
doom approaches. The Dead awaken;
for the hour is come for the oathbreakers:
at the Stone of Erech they shall stand again
and hear there a horn in the hills ringing.
Whose shall the horn be? Who shall call them
from the grey twilight, the forgotten people?
The heir of him to whom the oath they swore.
From the North shall he come, need shall drive him:
he shall pass the Door to the Paths of the Dead.
fulfil: T.A. 3019: Aragorn came from the North at need, passed the Door beneath the Dwimorberg, and blew Elrohir's silver horn at the Stone of Erech. Every line held.
note: Brought to Aragorn as a message from Elrond by the sons of Elrond: 'Bid Aragorn remember the words of the seer, and the Paths of the Dead.'

n: The summoning at the Stone of Erech
by: Aragorn son of Arathorn (Elessar), answered by the King of the Dead
on: the Oathbreakers
when: T.A. 3019, 8 March, at midnight
q:
'Oathbreakers, why have ye come?'
   And a voice was heard out of the night that answered him, as if from far away:
   'To fulfil our oath and have peace.'
   Then Aragorn said: 'The hour is come at last. Now I go to Pelargir upon Anduin, and ye shall come after me. And when all this land is clean of the servants of Sauron, I will hold the oath fulfilled, and ye shall have peace and depart for ever. For I am Elessar, Isildur's heir of Gondor.'
fulfil: At Pelargir the Dead took the black fleet without a blow struck; Aragorn released them, and 'they vanished'. Their coming up Anduin under Arnor's standard turned the Battle of the Pelennor Fields.

n: Glorfindel's prophecy: 'not by the hand of man will he fall'
on: the Witch-king of Angmar, Lord of the Nazgûl
when: T.A. 1975, after the Battle of Fornost
q: 'Eärnur now rode back, but Glorfindel, looking into the gathering dark, said: ''Do not pursue him! He will not return to this land. Far off yet is his doom, and not by the hand of man will he fall.'' These words many remembered; but Eärnur was angry, desiring only to be avenged for his disgrace.'
fulfil: 1,044 years later on the Pelennor the Witch-king was destroyed by Éowyn of Rohan and Meriadoc Brandybuck of the Shire — a woman and a hobbit, neither of them a Man.
note: Gandalf quotes it in the field a little differently: 'if words spoken of old be true, not by the hand of man shall he fall, and hidden from the Wise is the doom that awaits him' (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:36628). No text in the corpus contains the popular form 'no living man may kill me' as a prophecy — that phrasing is the Witch-king's own boast, not Glorfindel's.

n: 'But no living man am I!' — the ending of the Witch-king
by: the Lord of the Nazgûl, answered by Éowyn of Rohan
on: the Witch-king himself
when: T.A. 3019, 15 March, on the Pelennor Fields
q:
'Hinder me? Thou fool. No living man may hinder me!'
    Then Merry heard of all sounds in that hour the strangest. It seemed that Dernhelm laughed, and the clear voice was like the ring of steel. 'But no living man am I! You look upon a woman. Éowyn I am, Éomund's daughter. You stand between me and my lord and kin. Begone, if you be not deathless! For living or dark undead, I will smite you, if you touch him.'
fulfil: Merry's Barrow-blade broke the spell that knit his unseen sinews; Éowyn drove her sword between crown and mantle, 'and the crown rolled away with a clang'.
note: The boast and the prophecy meet exactly here. The corpus text has OCR-damaged accents on 'Éowyn' and 'Éomund'; restored in this quotation.

n: The words on the Ring
by: Sauron, in the Black Speech, graven in the Ring
on: the bearers of the Rings of Power, and all who wear the One
when: S.A. c. 1600, in Orodruin
q:
Ash nazg durbatulûk, ash nazg gimbatul, ash nazg thrakatulûk
                      agh burzum-ishi krimpatul.

One Ring to rule them all, One Ring to find them, One Ring to bring them all and in the Darkness bind them.
c: LotR II.2 (Black Speech and gloss); the whole verse at I.2
fulfil: Nine Kings of Men were bound and became the Nazgûl; the Seven were consumed or recovered by Sauron; only the Three, made without his hand, escaped — and they too failed when the One was unmade.
note: The full verse ('Three Rings for the Elven-kings under the sky…') stands at t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:2796 and as the epigraph at line 11. Elrond: 'Out of the Black Years come the words that the Smiths of Eregion heard, and knew that they had been betrayed.' The quotation joins the Black Speech inscription as Gandalf spoke it at the Council (line 11781) with his Common Tongue rendering a few lines later (line 11800); both are on the same page of the same speech.

n: Isildur's claim of the Ring as weregild
on: himself and his line
when: S.A. 3441, on the slopes of Orodruin
q: ' ''This I will have as weregild for my father, and my brother,'' he said; and therefore whether we would or no, he took it to treasure it. But soon he was betrayed by it to his death; and so it is named in the North Isildur's Bane.'
fulfil: Two years later the Ring slipped from his finger in the Gladden Fields and he was shot down by Orcs. The whole of the Third Age follows from this refusal.
note: Reported by Elrond, who stood by and counselled otherwise.

n: The dream-verse of Boromir and Faramir
by: a voice heard in dream, 'remote but clear'
on: Gondor, and the Halfling
when: T.A. 3018, before the assault on Osgiliath
q:
Seek for the Sword that was broken:
   In Imladris it dwells;
There shall be counsels taken
   Stronger than Morgul-spells.
There shall be shown a token
   That Doom is near at hand,
For Isildur's Bane shall waken,
   And the Halfling forth shall stand.
fulfil: At the Council of Elrond in Imladris the Sword that was Broken was shown, Isildur's Bane was named, and Frodo the Halfling stood forth: 'I will take the Ring, though I do not know the way.'
note: The dream came often to Faramir and once to Boromir; Boromir claimed the errand, which set his own doom in motion.

n: 'I will take the Ring, though I do not know the way'
on: himself
when: T.A. 3018, 25 October, at the Council of Elrond
q:
At last with an effort he spoke, and wondered to hear his own words, as if some other will was using his small voice.
    'I will take the Ring,' he said, 'though I do not know the way.'
fulfil: He carried it to the Cracks of Doom — and there, at the last step, claimed it instead: 'I do not choose now to do what I came to do.' The Quest was achieved by Gollum's teeth, not by Frodo's will.
note: Elrond's answer names it a doom: 'I think that this task is appointed for you, Frodo; and that if you do not find a way, no one will.' Frodo's dread beforehand is 'as if he was awaiting the pronouncement of some doom that he had long foreseen'.

n: 'No oath or bond is laid on you' — Elrond and the Fellowship
on: the Nine Walkers (refused)
when: T.A. 3018, 25 December, at Rivendell
q:
'The others go with him as free companions, to help him on his way. You may tarry, or come back, or turn aside into other paths, as chance allows. The further you go, the less easy will it be to withdraw; yet no oath or bond is laid on you to go further than you will. For you do not yet know the strength of your hearts, and you cannot foresee what each may meet upon the road.'
    'Faithless is he that says farewell when the road darkens,' said Gimli.
    'Maybe,' said Elrond, 'but let him not vow to walk in the dark, who has not seen the nightfall.'
    'Yet sworn word may strengthen quaking heart,' said Gimli.
    'Or break it,' said Elrond.
fulfil: The deliberate anti-oath of the legendarium, set against the Oath of Fëanor. Free of any bond, the Company held — except Boromir, who broke without having sworn.
note: This exchange is the moral hinge of the whole database: Tolkien's answer to the ruin worked by binding words.

n: 'You cannot pass'
on: the Balrog of Moria, Durin's Bane
when: T.A. 3019, 15 January, on the Bridge of Khazad-dûm
q: 'You cannot pass,' he said. The orcs stood still, and a dead silence fell. 'I am a servant of the Secret Fire, wielder of the flame of Anor. You cannot pass. The dark fire will not avail you, flame of Udûn. Go back to the Shadow! You cannot pass.'
fulfil: The Balrog did not pass. Gandalf broke the bridge — 'Fly, you fools!' — and both fell; Gandalf threw down his enemy on the peak of Zirakzigil and returned as Gandalf the White.
note: The corpus is unambiguous: the words are 'You cannot pass', repeated three times, and again at line 15162. 'You shall not pass' does not occur in this scene.

n: Gollum's oath on the Precious
by: Sméagol/Gollum, sworn by the One Ring
on: himself
when: T.A. 3019, in the Emyn Muil
q:
'Sméagol will swear never, never, to let Him have it. Never! Sméagol will save it. But he must swear on the Precious.'
   'No! not on it,' said Frodo, looking down at him with stern pity. 'All you wish is to see it and touch it, if you can, though you know it would drive you mad. Not on it. Swear by it, if you will. For you know where it is. Yes, you know, Sméagol. It is before you.'
…
   'We promises, yes I promise!' said Gollum. 'I will serve the master of the Precious. Good master, good Sméagol, gollum, gollum!'
fulfil: He broke it at Cirith Ungol, leading Frodo to Shelob. The oath took him at the very end: he seized the Ring on the brink of the Cracks of Doom and fell in with it — and so 'never, never, let Him have it' was kept, and the Quest achieved.
note: Frodo's warning is itself a curse-formula: 'Would you commit your promise to that, Sméagol? It will hold you. But it is more treacherous than you are. It may twist your words. Beware!' The ellipsis marks Frodo's intervening command 'Down! down!… Now speak your promise!'; all quoted words are contiguous in the scene.

n: Frodo's doom upon Gollum
by: Frodo Baggins, 'a figure robed in white… at its breast a wheel of fire'
on: Gollum
when: T.A. 3019, 25 March, on the slopes of Orodruin
q: 'Begone, and trouble me no more! If you touch me ever again, you shall be cast yourself into the Fire of Doom.'
fulfil: Within the hour Gollum touched him — bit the Ring from his finger in the Sammath Naur — and fell into the Fire of Doom with it. The one prophecy in the book fulfilled to the letter and within minutes.
note: Sam sees the moment with 'other vision'; the words come out of the fire, not simply out of Frodo.

n: Saruman's curse on the Shire
on: whoever should strike him, and the Shire itself
when: T.A. 3019, 3 November, at Bag End
q: 'But do not think that when I lost all my goods I lost all my power! Whoever strikes me shall be accursed. And if my blood stains the Shire, it shall wither and never again be healed.'
fulfil: Voided. Frodo forbade his killing three times over; Saruman was cut down not by a hobbit but by Wormtongue, and Frodo's mercy is what disarmed the curse. The Shire was healed the following spring by Galadriel's earth and the mallorn at the Party Field, and 1420 was the greatest year in living memory.
note: Saruman's earlier threat to Frodo is the more accurate one: 'I have already done much that you will find it hard to mend or undo in your lives.' Frodo's reply — 'It is useless to meet revenge with revenge: it will heal nothing' — is the counter-formula.

n: The Oath of Eorl
by: Eorl the Young, King of the Éothéod
on: himself and all his heirs in the new land
when: T.A. 2510, on the Halifirien at the tomb of Elendil
q: Hear now all peoples who bow not to the Shadow in the East, by the gift of the Lord of the Mundburg we will come to dwell in the land that he names Calenardhon, and therefore I vow in my own name and on behalf of the Éothéod of the North that between us and the Great People of the West there shall be friendship for ever: their enemies shall be our enemies, their need shall be our need, and whatsoever evil, or threat, or assault may come upon them we will aid them to the utmost end of our strength. This vow shall descend to my heirs, all such as may come after me in our new land, and let them keep it in faith unbroken, lest the Shadow fall upon them and they become accursed.
fulfil: Kept unbroken for 509 years. Théoden answered the Red Arrow and rode to the Pelennor at the summons of Gondor in T.A. 3019, and died fulfilling it: 'Arise, arise, Riders of Théoden!'
note: 'There seems not to be any other version of the Oath of Eorl extant apart from that in the text' (Christopher Tolkien, UT notes, line 9992).

n: The Oath of Cirion
on: Gondor, in answer to Eorl
when: T.A. 2510, on the Halifirien
q:
Vanda sina termaruva Elenna·nóreo alcar enyalien ar Elendil Vorondo voronwë. Nai tiruvantes i hárar mahalmassen mi Númen ar i Eru i or ilyë mahalmar eä tennoio.

And again he said in the Common Speech:

This oath shall stand in memory of the glory of the Land of the Star, and of the faith of Elendil the Faithful, in the keeping of those who sit upon the thrones of the West and of the One who is above all thrones for ever.
fulfil: Held on both sides to the end of the Third Age and beyond; Aragorn confirmed the gift of Calenardhon to Éomer in the Fourth Age.
note: One of the very few invocations of Eru in the whole of The Lord of the Rings and its apparatus. 'Such an oath had not been heard in Middle-earth since Elendil himself had sworn alliance with Gil-galad King of the Eldar.' The Quenya line carries an OCR-damaged 'ea¨' and a footnote marker '43' in this corpus, restored to 'eä' here.

n: 'The hands of the king are the hands of a healer'
by: Ioreth, wise-woman of Gondor, quoted by Gandalf
on: the rightful King of Gondor
when: T.A. 3019, 15 March, in the Houses of Healing
q: 'Thus spake Ioreth, wise-woman of Gondor: The hands of the king are the hands of a healer, and so shall the rightful king be known.'
fulfil: Aragorn recalled Faramir, Éowyn and Merry from the Black Breath with athelas that night, and the word went out through the City that the King had come again.
note: Ioreth speaks it as old lore, not her own foresight; Gandalf uses it deliberately to make Aragorn's claim before the people.

n: The parting of Aragorn and Arwen
on: themselves
when: F.A. 120, in the House of the Kings, Minas Tirith
q:
'Lady Undómiel,' said Aragorn, 'the hour is indeed hard, yet it was made even in that day when we met under the white birches in the garden of Elrond where none now walk. And on the hill of Cerin Amroth when we forsook both the Shadow and the Twilight this doom we accepted… I am the last of the Númenóreans and the latest King of the Elder Days; and to me has been given not only a span thrice that of Men of Middle-earth, but also the grace to go at my will, and give back the gift. Now, therefore, I will sleep.
   'I speak no comfort to you, for there is no comfort for such pain within the circles of the world. The uttermost choice is before you: to repent and go to the Havens and bear away into the West the memory of our days together that shall there be evergreen but never more than memory; or else to abide the Doom of Men.'
    'Nay, dear lord,' she said, 'that choice is long over. There is now no ship that would bear me hence, and I must indeed abide the Doom of Men, whether I will or I nill: the loss and the silence… For if this is indeed, as the Eldar say, the gift of the One to Men, it is bitter to receive.'
    'So it seems,' he said. 'But let us not be overthrown at the final test, who of old renounced the Shadow and the Ring. In sorrow we must go, but not in despair. Behold! we are not bound for ever to the circles of the world, and beyond them is more than memory. Farewell!'
    'Estel, Estel!' she cried
c: LotR Appendix A I(v), 'The Tale of Aragorn and Arwen'
fulfil: Aragorn gave back the gift and died; Arwen went to Lórien and 'laid herself to rest upon Cerin Amroth' in the year after. The Doom of Men, chosen and abided.
note: The corpus text carries a page-header artifact ('APPENDIX A 1063') across line 47672, removed here; two short passages elided at the ellipses; and Appendix A's doubled quotation marks (a tale within Bilbo's book) normalised to single.

n: The Second Prophecy of Mandos (Quenta version)
on: Morgoth, Túrin, the Silmarils, and the end of Arda
when: after the Great Battle, at the end of the First Age
q:
Thus spake the prophecy of Mandos, which he declared in Valmar at the judgement of the Gods, and the rumour of it was whispered among all the Elves of the West: when the world is old and the Powers grow weary, then Morgoth shall come back through the Door out of the Timeless Night; and he shall destroy the Sun and the Moon, but Earendel shall come upon him as a white flame and drive him from the airs. Then shall the last battle be gathered on the fields of Valinor. In that day Tulkas shall strive with Melko, and on his right shall stand Fionwe and on his left Turin Turambar, son of Hurin, Conqueror of Fate, and it shall be the black sword of Turin that deals unto Melko his death and final end; and so shall the children of Hurin and all Men be avenged.

Thereafter shall the Silmarils be recovered out of sea and earth and air; for Earendel shall descend and yield up that flame that he hath had in keeping. Then Feanor shall bear the Three and yield them unto Yavanna Palurien; and she will break them and with their fire rekindle the Two Trees, and a great light shall come forth; and the Mountains of Valinor shall be levelled, so that the light goes out all over the world. In that light the Gods will again grow young, and the Elves awake and all their dead arise, and the purpose of Iluvatar be fulfilled concerning them. But of Men in that day the prophecy speaks not, save of Turin only, and him it names among the Gods.
fulfil: Never; it is a prophecy of the Dagor Dagorath, the Last Battle, beyond the end of the recorded histories.
note: ABANDONED. Tolkien later dropped this entirely. Christopher Tolkien: 'The Second Prophecy of Mandos (V.333) had now therefore definitively disappeared' (t1_10_morgoths_ring.txt:7845). The Valaquenta texts that replaced it end instead: 'if any change shall come and the Marring be amended, Manwë and Varda may know; but they have not revealed it, and it is not declared in the dooms of Mandos.' That passage was used to close the published Silmarillion. Do not read this entry as current legendarium. The corpus text has a stray page artifact ('j') breaking the sentence 'Then shall the last battle be gathered on j / the fields of Valinor'; removed here.

n: The Second Prophecy of Mandos (Quenta Silmarillion version)
on: Morgoth, Túrin, the Silmarils, and the remaking of Arda
when: after the Great Battle, at the end of the First Age
q:
Thus spake Mandos in prophecy, when the Gods sat in judgement in Valinor, and the rumour of his words was whispered among all the Elves of the West. When the world is old and the Powers grow weary, then Morgoth, seeing that the guard sleepeth, shall come back through the Door of Night out of the Timeless Void; and he shall destroy the Sun and Moon. But Earendel shall descend upon him as a white and searing flame and drive him from the airs. Then shall the Last Battle be gathered on the fields of Valinor. In that day Tulkas shall strive with Morgoth, and on his right hand shall be Fionwe, and on his left Turin Turambar, son of Hurin, coming from the halls of Mandos; and the black sword of Turin shall deal unto Morgoth his death and final end; and so shall the children of Hurin and all Men be avenged.

Thereafter shall Earth be broken and re-made, and the Silmarils shall be recovered out of Air and Earth and Sea; for Earendel shall descend and surrender that flame which he hath had in keeping. Then Feanor shall take the Three Jewels and bear them to Yavanna Palurien; and she will break them and with their fire rekindle the Two Trees, and a great light shall come forth. And the Mountains of Valinor shall be levelled, so that the Light shall go out over all the world. In that light the Gods will grow young again, and the Elves awake and all their dead arise, and the purpose of Iluvatar be fulfilled concerning them. But of Men in that day the prophecy of Mandos doth not speak, and no Man it names, save Turin only, and to him a place is given among the sons of the Valar.
fulfil: Never; and by Tolkien's later intention, never to be.
note: ABANDONED, like second_prophecy_mandos_q. This is the fuller and later of the two texts (c. 1937), but it too was dropped in the 1950s revisions; see HoME X, t1_10_morgoths_ring.txt:7845. It appears nowhere in the published Silmarillion. Recorded here because it is the source of every later reference to the Dagor Dagorath and to Túrin's return, but it is superseded material, not current.
